1:
"And the chief fathers of the families of the children of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh, of the families of the sons of Joseph, came near, and spake before Moses and before the princes..." The witnesses note the heads of Gilead, of Manasseh, coming with a humble address about the daughters of Zelophehad. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were the princes of the families of Gilead, of the tribe of Manasseh, whose grandson Gilead was. The witnesses note that Henry marks the humble address the heads of Manasseh made to Moses and the princes, on occasion of the late order concerning Zelophehad's daughters. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the chief fathers — the heads of Manasseh; the came near — before Moses and the princes; the a real difficulty — raised by the grant to the daughters; the lawful petition — the seemly way to seek redress.

The witnesses agree the chapter opens with an orderly and respectful appeal: the heads of Manasseh, kinsmen of Zelophehad, brought their concern before Moses and the princes, not murmuring but seeking a right settlement of a real difficulty raised by the earlier grant to the daughters; the manner of their coming — humble, public, and submissive to the leaders — shows the seemly way to seek redress, by lawful petition rather than complaint, and prepares for the just answer God would give.

So the witnesses make the verse the humble appeal: The witnesses note the the chief fathers: the heads of Manasseh. The witnesses note the came near: before Moses and the princes. The witnesses note the a real difficulty: raised by the grant to the daughters. The witnesses note the lawful petition: the seemly way to seek redress. The expositors observe the the chief fathers: the heads of Manasseh. The expositors observe the came near: before Moses and the princes. The expositors observe the a real difficulty: raised by the grant to the daughters. The expositors observe the lawful petition: the seemly way to seek redress. The believer is taught to mark the the chief fathers, the came near, the a real difficulty, the lawful petition, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that the heads of Manasseh came humbly before Moses about Zelophehad's daughters — the seemly way to seek redress by lawful petition, preparing for the just answer God would give.
2:
"And they said, The LORD commanded my lord to give the land for an inheritance by lot to the children of Israel: and my lord was commanded by the LORD to give the inheritance of Zelophehad our brother unto his daughters." The witnesses note their owning both the command to divide the land by lot and the grant of Zelophehad's inheritance to his daughters. The witnesses note that Gill notes one spoke in the name of the rest. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set their statement — the LORD commanded the land given by lot, and the inheritance of Zelophehad given to his daughters. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the land by lot — the LORD's first command; the to Zelophehad's daughters — the grant of his inheritance; the owning both — reverently before raising their difficulty; the a right spirit — not disputing but seeking a settlement.

The witnesses agree the petitioners began by owning the rightness of both earlier commands: that the land be divided by lot, and that Zelophehad's daughters inherit their father's portion; their reverent acknowledgment of God's former word, before raising their difficulty, shows a right spirit — not disputing the grant already made to the daughters, but seeking how it might stand without harm to the tribe, submitting their question to the authority that had given the law.

So the witnesses make the verse owning the commands: The witnesses note the the land by lot: the LORD's first command. The witnesses note the to Zelophehad's daughters: the grant of his inheritance. The witnesses note the owning both: reverently before raising their difficulty. The witnesses note the a right spirit: not disputing but seeking a settlement. The expositors observe the the land by lot: the LORD's first command. The expositors observe the to Zelophehad's daughters: the grant of his inheritance. The expositors observe the owning both: reverently before raising their difficulty. The expositors observe the a right spirit: not disputing but seeking a settlement. The believer is taught to mark the the land by lot, the to Zelophehad's daughters, the owning both, the a right spirit, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that they owned both the dividing of the land by lot and the grant to Zelophehad's daughters — a right spirit, not disputing God's word but seeking how the grant might stand without harm.
3:
"And if they be married to any of the sons of the other tribes of the children of Israel, then shall their inheritance be taken from the inheritance of our fathers..." The witnesses note the difficulty raised — if the daughters marry into other tribes, their inheritance passes from Manasseh. The witnesses note that Gill notes the supposition was not unreasonable, and perhaps likely, from the application of young men of other tribes to them. The witnesses note that the inheritance would then be added to the tribe into which they married, and taken from their own. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the married to other tribes — the heiresses wedding outside; the inheritance taken — their land passing with them; the Manasseh the poorer — its portion diminished; the a real question — the tribal division at stake.

The witnesses agree the difficulty was real and well stated: should the heiresses marry men of other tribes, their land would pass with them to those tribes, and Manasseh be the poorer, its inheritance diminished by the marriage; the concern was not selfish but for the right ordering of the tribal portions, that the careful division of the land not be undone by the marrying of heiresses — a genuine question of justice the petitioners rightly brought to be resolved.

So the witnesses make the verse the difficulty: The witnesses note the married to other tribes: the heiresses wedding outside. The witnesses note the inheritance taken: their land passing with them. The witnesses note the Manasseh the poorer: its portion diminished. The witnesses note the a real question: the tribal division at stake. The expositors observe the married to other tribes: the heiresses wedding outside. The expositors observe the inheritance taken: their land passing with them. The expositors observe the Manasseh the poorer: its portion diminished. The expositors observe the a real question: the tribal division at stake. The believer is taught to mark the married to other tribes, the inheritance taken, the Manasseh the poorer, the a real question, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that if the daughters married into other tribes their inheritance would pass from Manasseh — a real question of justice, lest the careful division of the land be undone by the marrying of heiresses.
4:
"And when the jubilee of the children of Israel shall be, then shall their inheritance be put unto the inheritance of the tribe whereunto they are received: so shall their inheritance be taken away..." The witnesses note that even the jubilee would not remedy this, but fix the inheritance in the tribe into which they married. The witnesses note that Gill notes the jubilee, when inheritances returned to their original owners, would here be of no service but rather the contrary, fixing the daughters' land in another tribe. The witnesses note that so their inheritance would be taken from their own tribe. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the jubilee — restoring sold land to its owners; the of no service here — land passed by marriage not returning; the fixed in the new tribe — the loss made permanent; the a special remedy needed — the jubilee not sufficing.

The witnesses agree the petitioners foresaw that the usual safeguard would here fail: the jubilee, which restored sold land to its first owners, would not bring back land that had passed by marriage, but rather confirm it in the new tribe — so the loss to Manasseh would be permanent; the keenness of the difficulty, that the very law meant to protect inheritances could not protect this one, shows the wisdom of bringing it to God for a special remedy, which the jubilee alone could not supply.

So the witnesses make the verse even the jubilee: The witnesses note the the jubilee: restoring sold land to its owners. The witnesses note the of no service here: land passed by marriage not returning. The witnesses note the fixed in the new tribe: the loss made permanent. The witnesses note the a special remedy needed: the jubilee not sufficing. The expositors observe the the jubilee: restoring sold land to its owners. The expositors observe the of no service here: land passed by marriage not returning. The expositors observe the fixed in the new tribe: the loss made permanent. The expositors observe the a special remedy needed: the jubilee not sufficing. The believer is taught to mark the the jubilee, the of no service here, the fixed in the new tribe, the a special remedy needed, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that even the jubilee would fix the inheritance in the new tribe, not restore it — the usual safeguard failing here, a special remedy needed that the jubilee alone could not supply.
5:
"And Moses commanded the children of Israel according to the word of the LORD, saying, The tribe of the sons of Joseph hath said well." The witnesses note Moses, by God's word, approving the petition — the tribe of Joseph had spoken rightly. The witnesses note that Gill notes the law concerned all the tribes of Israel, not Manasseh only. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Moses declared what they had affirmed was right. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses commanded — by the word of the LORD; the the sons of Joseph — the petitioners of Manasseh; the hath said well — their concern owned as just; the a law for all — binding every tribe, not Manasseh only.

The witnesses agree God owned the justice of the appeal: Moses, speaking by the word of the LORD, declared that "the sons of Joseph have said well," their concern for the tribal inheritance being right and worthy of a remedy; the ready approval shows that God hears the reasonable petitions of his people and provides for real difficulties, the lawful and humble appeal answered with a just and gracious law — and the resulting statute made to bind all the tribes, not Manasseh alone.

So the witnesses make the verse said well: The witnesses note the Moses commanded: by the word of the LORD. The witnesses note the the sons of Joseph: the petitioners of Manasseh. The witnesses note the hath said well: their concern owned as just. The witnesses note the a law for all: binding every tribe, not Manasseh only. The expositors observe the Moses commanded: by the word of the LORD. The expositors observe the the sons of Joseph: the petitioners of Manasseh. The expositors observe the hath said well: their concern owned as just. The expositors observe the a law for all: binding every tribe, not Manasseh only. The believer is taught to mark the Moses commanded, the the sons of Joseph, the hath said well, the a law for all, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that Moses, by God's word, approved the petition — "the sons of Joseph have said well" — God hearing the reasonable appeal of his people and giving a just law to bind all the tribes.
6:
"This is the thing which the LORD doth command concerning the daughters of Zelophehad, saying, Let them marry to whom they think best; only to the family of the tribe of their father shall they marry." The witnesses note the remedy — the daughters free to marry whom they would, but only within their father's tribe. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Targum of Jonathan limits this order to the daughters of Zelophehad, or to what might happen before the land was divided. The witnesses note that they might marry to whom they thought best, only within the tribe of their father. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD doth command — the appointed provision; the marry whom they think best — their freedom of choice kept; the only within their tribe — the inheritance secured to Manasseh; the liberty with order — honouring both their right and the tribe's.

The witnesses agree the remedy joined liberty with order: the daughters kept their freedom to choose their husbands "to whom they think best," yet were bound to marry within their own tribe, that their inheritance stay in Manasseh; the wisdom of the law honoured both the daughters' right and the tribe's, restraining their choice no further than the inheritance required — a gentle and just provision, securing the land without robbing them of consent.

So the witnesses make the verse the remedy: The witnesses note the the LORD doth command: the appointed provision. The witnesses note the marry whom they think best: their freedom of choice kept. The witnesses note the only within their tribe: the inheritance secured to Manasseh. The witnesses note the liberty with order: honouring both their right and the tribe's. The expositors observe the the LORD doth command: the appointed provision. The expositors observe the marry whom they think best: their freedom of choice kept. The expositors observe the only within their tribe: the inheritance secured to Manasseh. The expositors observe the liberty with order: honouring both their right and the tribe's. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD doth command, the marry whom they think best, the only within their tribe, the liberty with order, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that the daughters might marry whom they would, but only within their father's tribe — liberty joined with order, securing the inheritance without robbing them of consent.
7:
"So shall not the inheritance of the children of Israel remove from tribe to tribe: for every one of the children of Israel shall keep himself to the inheritance of the tribe of his fathers." The witnesses note the principle — the inheritance was not to pass from tribe to tribe, each keeping his fathers' portion. The witnesses note that Gill notes this shows the law concerned all the tribes, that none's inheritance should remove to another. The witnesses note that every Israelite was to keep to the inheritance of his fathers' tribe. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not remove from tribe to tribe — the land staying with each; the keep to the inheritance — every man his fathers' portion; the the order preserved — god's division kept stable; the the unchanging inheritance — pointing to that which fades not (1 Pet. 1:4).

The witnesses agree the law guarded the permanence of the tribal inheritances: the land allotted to each tribe was to remain with it, not drifting from one to another by marriage, every man keeping his fathers' portion; the fixedness of the inheritance preserved the order God had set in dividing the land, and kept each tribe in its place — a stability that pointed to the unchanging inheritance God gives his people, which "fadeth not away" and passes not from hand to hand (cf. 1 Pet. 1:4).

So the witnesses make the verse the inheritance fixed: The witnesses note the not remove from tribe to tribe: the land staying with each. The witnesses note the keep to the inheritance: every man his fathers' portion. The witnesses note the the order preserved: God's division kept stable. The witnesses note the the unchanging inheritance: pointing to that which fades not (1 Pet. 1:4). The expositors observe the not remove from tribe to tribe: the land staying with each. The expositors observe the keep to the inheritance: every man his fathers' portion. The expositors observe the the order preserved: God's division kept stable. The expositors observe the the unchanging inheritance: pointing to that which fades not (1 Pet. 1:4). The believer is taught to mark the not remove from tribe to tribe, the keep to the inheritance, the the order preserved, the the unchanging inheritance, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that the inheritance was not to remove from tribe to tribe, each keeping his fathers' portion — the order of God's division preserved, a stability pointing to the unchanging inheritance that fades not away (1 Pet. 1:4).
8:
"And every daughter, that possesseth an inheritance in any tribe of the children of Israel, shall be wife unto one of the family of the tribe of her father..." The witnesses note the law made general — every heiress in Israel to marry within her own tribe. The witnesses note that Gill notes the same law that gave Zelophehad's daughters their right gave every daughter where there were no sons a right to inherit, and every such heiress was bound by this rule. The witnesses note that Clarke notes every daughter possessing an inheritance was so bound. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the every daughter — that possesseth an inheritance; the in any tribe — the rule made general; the wife within her tribe — her land kept in it; the a lasting law — daughters' right established, every tribe secured.

The witnesses agree the particular case became a general statute: not Zelophehad's daughters only but every heiress in Israel — a daughter inheriting where there were no sons — was to marry within her father's tribe, that her land stay in it; the broadening of the rule shows how God, from a single difficulty, framed a lasting law for the good of all, the right of daughters to inherit established and the inheritance of every tribe alike secured.

So the witnesses make the verse the general law: The witnesses note the every daughter: that possesseth an inheritance. The witnesses note the in any tribe: the rule made general. The witnesses note the wife within her tribe: her land kept in it. The witnesses note the a lasting law: daughters' right established, every tribe secured. The expositors observe the every daughter: that possesseth an inheritance. The expositors observe the in any tribe: the rule made general. The expositors observe the wife within her tribe: her land kept in it. The expositors observe the a lasting law: daughters' right established, every tribe secured. The believer is taught to mark the every daughter, the in any tribe, the wife within her tribe, the a lasting law, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that every heiress in Israel was to marry within her own tribe — the particular case made a general statute, daughters' right to inherit established and every tribe's inheritance secured.
9:
"Neither shall the inheritance remove from one tribe to another tribe; but every one of the tribes of the children of Israel shall keep himself to his own inheritance." The witnesses note the principle restated — no inheritance to pass between tribes, each keeping its own. The witnesses note that Gill notes the jubilee alone did not sufficiently secure this without this law, as the case showed. The witnesses note that every tribe was to keep to its own inheritance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not remove — no inheritance passing between tribes; the keep to his own — each tribe holding its land; the beyond the jubilee — the further law needed to secure it; the order and stability — the portions held firm through the generations.

The witnesses agree the repetition seals the great principle of the chapter: the inheritances were to stay fixed within their tribes, the jubilee not enough by itself to secure them, this further law needed to keep each tribe's land its own; the careful guarding of the boundaries shows God's concern for order and stability in his people's life, that the portions he had assigned be held firm through the generations, none encroaching on another, the inheritance kept whole.

So the witnesses make the verse kept within the tribe: The witnesses note the not remove: no inheritance passing between tribes. The witnesses note the keep to his own: each tribe holding its land. The witnesses note the beyond the jubilee: the further law needed to secure it. The witnesses note the order and stability: the portions held firm through the generations. The expositors observe the not remove: no inheritance passing between tribes. The expositors observe the keep to his own: each tribe holding its land. The expositors observe the beyond the jubilee: the further law needed to secure it. The expositors observe the order and stability: the portions held firm through the generations. The believer is taught to mark the not remove, the keep to his own, the beyond the jubilee, the order and stability, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that no inheritance was to pass between tribes, each keeping its own — God's concern for order and stability, the portions he assigned held firm through the generations.
10:
"Even as the LORD commanded Moses, so did the daughters of Zelophehad:" The witnesses note the daughters' ready obedience — they did just as the LORD had commanded. The witnesses note that Gill notes they married into the family of their father's tribe, according to the account that follows. The witnesses note that their obedience was exact to the command. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as the LORD commanded — the condition God set; the so did the daughters — their exact compliance; the within their tribe — marrying as bidden; the the right use of a right — bowing to the law for the common good.

The witnesses agree the daughters' obedience crowns the matter with a fair example: having sought and won their right, they yet submitted readily to the condition God set, marrying within their own tribe as he commanded; their cheerful compliance — not standing on their liberty to the hurt of the tribe, but bowing to the law for the common good — shows the right use of a granted right, and the beauty of an obedience that asks no more than God allows and does just as he bids.

So the witnesses make the verse the daughters' obedience: The witnesses note the as the LORD commanded: the condition God set. The witnesses note the so did the daughters: their exact compliance. The witnesses note the within their tribe: marrying as bidden. The witnesses note the the right use of a right: bowing to the law for the common good. The expositors observe the as the LORD commanded: the condition God set. The expositors observe the so did the daughters: their exact compliance. The expositors observe the within their tribe: marrying as bidden. The expositors observe the the right use of a right: bowing to the law for the common good. The believer is taught to mark the as the LORD commanded, the so did the daughters, the within their tribe, the the right use of a right, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that the daughters did just as the LORD commanded — a fair example of obedience, the right use of a granted right, bowing to the law for the common good.
11:
"For Mahlah, Tirzah, and Hoglah, and Milcah, and Noah, the daughters of Zelophehad, were married unto their father's brothers' sons:" The witnesses note the five daughters named, married to their cousins within the tribe. The witnesses note that Gill notes the names are the same as before, the order a little varied — here, Aben Ezra thinks, according to their marriage, or, Jarchi says, to show they were equal to one another. The witnesses note that they married their father's brothers' sons. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, Noah — the five named; the their father's brothers' sons — married to their cousins; the within the family — keeping the land in the nearest line; the honoured by name — remembered in the sacred record.

The witnesses agree the naming of the five and their marriage to kinsmen completes the obedient fulfilment: each daughter is honoured by name, and all married within the family, to their cousins, keeping the inheritance in the nearest line; the careful record shows that these faithful women, whose bold and believing claim had won a right for all the daughters of Israel, are remembered individually in the sacred history, their names preserved as those who both sought their portion in faith and kept it in obedience.

So the witnesses make the verse the five daughters: The witnesses note the Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, Noah: the five named. The witnesses note the their father's brothers' sons: married to their cousins. The witnesses note the within the family: keeping the land in the nearest line. The witnesses note the honoured by name: remembered in the sacred record. The expositors observe the Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, Noah: the five named. The expositors observe the their father's brothers' sons: married to their cousins. The expositors observe the within the family: keeping the land in the nearest line. The expositors observe the honoured by name: remembered in the sacred record. The believer is taught to mark the Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, Noah, the their father's brothers' sons, the within the family, the honoured by name, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that the five daughters married their cousins within the tribe — each honoured by name, the faithful women remembered who sought their portion in faith and kept it in obedience.
12:
"And they were married into the families of the sons of Manasseh the son of Joseph, and their inheritance remained in the tribe of the family of their father." The witnesses note the daughters married within Manasseh, their inheritance staying in their father's tribe. The witnesses note that Gill notes they married into the family of the Hepherites, of which they were; their marrying into families (not one family) shows their cousins were not all sons of one man. The witnesses note that their inheritance remained in the tribe of their father. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the married into Manasseh — within their father's tribe; the the Hepherites — their own family; the inheritance remained — kept in their father's tribe; the every claim met — the daughters provided, the portion whole.

The witnesses agree the outcome answered the whole design of the law: the daughters married within Manasseh, and so their inheritance "remained in the tribe of the family of their father," exactly as God had purposed; the happy result — the daughters provided for, their right honoured, and the tribal portion kept whole — shows the wisdom and goodness of God's provision, which met every claim and harmed none, the inheritance preserved and the faithful daughters settled in their own.

So the witnesses make the verse the inheritance kept: The witnesses note the married into Manasseh: within their father's tribe. The witnesses note the the Hepherites: their own family. The witnesses note the inheritance remained: kept in their father's tribe. The witnesses note the every claim met: the daughters provided, the portion whole. The expositors observe the married into Manasseh: within their father's tribe. The expositors observe the the Hepherites: their own family. The expositors observe the inheritance remained: kept in their father's tribe. The expositors observe the every claim met: the daughters provided, the portion whole. The believer is taught to mark the married into Manasseh, the the Hepherites, the inheritance remained, the every claim met, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that the daughters married within Manasseh, their inheritance remaining in their father's tribe — God's provision answered, every claim met and none harmed, the portion kept whole.
13:
"These are the commandments and the judgments, which the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses unto the children of Israel in the plains of Moab by Jordan near Jericho." The witnesses note the closing words of the book — the commandments given by Moses in the plains of Moab, on the edge of the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes these are the judicial laws concerning the division of the land, inheritances, and the cities of refuge, commanded by the hand of Moses in the plains of Moab by Jordan near Jericho. The witnesses note that this closes the book, the Israelites being now on the very border of Canaan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the commandments and judgments — the laws here gathered up; the by the hand of Moses — given through the mediator; the in the plains of Moab — by Jordan near Jericho; the the threshold of the land — the wandering ended, the people ready.

The witnesses agree the verse fitly seals the whole book of Numbers: it gathers up "the commandments and the judgments" given through Moses in the plains of Moab, with Jordan and Jericho in view, the people poised at last on the threshold of the promised land; the close marks the end of the long wilderness journey and the giving of the laws for the life to come in Canaan, the book ending where the wandering ends — at the very edge of the inheritance, the word of God complete and the people ready to go over.

So the witnesses make the verse the close of the book: The witnesses note the the commandments and judgments: the laws here gathered up. The witnesses note the by the hand of Moses: given through the mediator. The witnesses note the in the plains of Moab: by Jordan near Jericho. The witnesses note the the threshold of the land: the wandering ended, the people ready. The expositors observe the the commandments and judgments: the laws here gathered up. The expositors observe the by the hand of Moses: given through the mediator. The expositors observe the in the plains of Moab: by Jordan near Jericho. The expositors observe the the threshold of the land: the wandering ended, the people ready. The believer is taught to mark the the commandments and judgments, the by the hand of Moses, the in the plains of Moab, the the threshold of the land, and to trust the God who orders his people's inheritance. The verse thus records that the book closes with the commandments given through Moses in the plains of Moab — the long wilderness journey ended, the people poised at the very edge of the promised inheritance, the word of God complete.
